full_notes:
Excretory Products and Their Elimination is decoded here only from the NCERT chapter text. The chapter begins with its own framing, examples and questions, then builds the topic through source-backed facts. The notes below keep that order: first the opening problem, then the important terms, then the mechanisms, examples and exam-useful connections that appear in the chapter itself.

The text explains that 239 239 CHEMICAL COORDINATION AND INTEGRATION You have already learnt that the neural system pr ovides a point-to-point rapid coordination among organs.

The text explains that the neural coordination is fast but short-lived.

The text explains that as the nerve fibres do not innervate all cells of the body and the cellular functions need to be continuously regulated; a special kind of coordination and integration has to be provided.

The text explains that the neural system and the endocrine system jointly coordinate and regulate the physiological functions in the body.

The text explains that 19.1 ENDOCRINE GLANDS AND HORMONES Endocrine glands lack ducts and are hence, called ductless glands.

The text explains that the classical definition of hormone as a chemical produced by endocrine glands and released into the blood and transported to a distantly located target organ has current scientific definition as follows: Hormones are non-nutrient chemicals which act as intercellular messengers and are produced in trace amounts.

The text explains that the new definition covers a number of new molecules in addition to the hormones secreted by the organised endocrine glands.

The text explains that invertebrates possess very simple endocrine systems with few hormones whereas a large number of chemicals act as hormones and provide coordination in the vertebrates.

The text explains that the human endocrine system is described here.

The text explains that cHEMICAL COORDINATION AND INTEGRATION CHAPTER 19 19.1 Endocrine Glands and Hormones 19.2 Human Endocrine System 19.3 Hormones of Heart, Kidney and Gastrointestinal Tract 19.4 Mechanism of Hormone Action

The text explains that bIOLOGY 240 19.2 HUMAN ENDOCRINE SYSTEM The endocrine glands and hormone producing diffused tissues/cells located in different parts of our body constitute the endocrine system.

The text explains that pituitary, pineal, thyroid, adrenal, pancreas, parathyroid, thymus and gonads (testis in males and ovary in females) are the organised endocrine bodies in our body (Figure 19.1).

The text explains that in addition to these, some other organs, e.g., gastrointestinal tract, liver, kidney, heart also produce hormones.

The text explains that a brief account of the structure and functions of all major endocrine glands and hypothalamus of the human body is given in the following sections.

The text explains that 19.2.1 The Hypothalamus As you know, the hypothalamus is the basal part of diencephalon, forebrain (Figure 19.1) and it regulates a wide spectrum of body functions.

The text explains that it contains several groups of neurosecretory cells called nuclei which produce hormones.

The text explains that these hormones reach the pituitary gland through a portal circulatory system and regulate the functions of the anterior pituitary.

The text explains that the posterior pituitary is under the direct neural regulation of the hypothalamus (Figure 19.2).

The text explains that the disease is hard to diagnose in the early stages and often goes undetected for many years, until changes in external features become noticeable.

The text explains that for example, it helps in maintaining the normal rhythms of sleep-wake cycle, body temperature.

The text explains that these hormones also support the process of red blood cell formation.

The text explains that pTH acts on bones and stimulates the process of bone resorption (dissolution/ demineralisation).

The text explains that 19.2.6 Thymus The thymus gland is a lobular structure located between lungs behind sternum on the ventral side of aorta.

The text explains that the thymus plays a major role in the development of the immune system.

The text explains that corticoids, which regulate the balance of water and electrolytes in our body are called mineralocorticoids.

The text explains that aldosterone is the main mineralocorticoid in our body.

The text explains that cortisol is also involved in maintaining the cardio-vascular system as well as the kidney functions.

The text explains that thus, aldosterone helps in the maintenance of electrolytes, body fluid volume, osmotic pressure and blood pressure.

The text explains that in addition, this hormone stimulates the process of gluconeogenesis which also contributes to hyperglycemia.

The text explains that androgens play a major stimulatory role in the process of spermatogenesis (formation of spermatozoa).

The text explains that androgens act on the central neural system and influence the male sexual behaviour (libido).

The text explains that after ovulation, the ruptured follicle is converted to a structure called corpus luteum, which secretes mainly progesterone.

The text explains that progesterone also acts on the mammary glands and stimulates the formation of alveoli (sac-like structures which store milk) and milk secretion.

The text explains that for example, the atrial wall of our heart secretes a very important peptide hormone called atrial natriuretic factor (ANF), which decreases blood pressure.

The text explains that when blood pressure is increased, ANF is secreted which causes dilation of the blood vessels.

The text explains that the endocrine system is composed of hypothalamus, pituitary and pineal, thyroid, adrenal, pancreas, parathyroid, thymus and gonads (testis and ovary).

The text explains that pineal gland secretes melatonin, which plays a very important role in the regulation of 24-hour (diurnal) rhythms of our body (e.g., rhythms of sleep and state of being awake, body temperature, etc.).

The text explains that the thyroid gland hormones play an important role in the regulation of the basal metabolic rate, development and maturation of the central neural system, erythropoiesis, metabolism of carbohydrates, proteins and fats, menstrual cycle.

The text explains that the adrenal cortex secretes glucocorticoids and mineralocorticoids.

The text explains that glucocorticoids stimulate gluconeogenesis, lipolysis, proteolysis, erythropoiesis, cardio-vascular system, blood pressure, and glomerular filtration rate and inhibit inflammatory reactions by suppressing the immune response.

The text explains that mineralocorticoids regulate water and electrolyte contents of the body.

The text explains that the atrial wall of the heart produces atrial natriuretic factor which decreases the blood pressure.

The text explains that give example(s) of: (a) Hyperglycemic hormone and hypoglycemic hormone (b) Hypercalcemic hormone (c) Gonadotrophic hormones (d) Progestational hormone (e) Blood pressure lowering hormone (f) Androgens and estrogens 7.

The text explains that 251 251 CHEMICAL COORDINATION AND INTEGRATION EXERCISES 1.

The text explains that define the following: (a) Exocrine gland (b) Endocrine gland (c) Hormone 2.

The text explains that diagrammatically indicate the location of the various endocrine glands in our body.

The text explains that list the hormones secreted by the following: (a) Hypothalamus (b) Pituitary (c) Thyroid (d) Parathyroid (e) Adrenal (f) Pancreas (g) Testis (h) Ovary (i) Thymus (j) Atrium (k) Kidney (l) G-I Tract 4.

The text explains that fill in the blanks: Hormones Target gland (a) Hypothalamic hormones __________________ (b) Thyrotrophin (TSH) __________________ (c) Corticotrophin (ACTH) __________________ (d) Gonadotrophins (LH, FSH) __________________ (e) Melanotrophin (MSH) __________________ 5.

The text explains that write short notes on the functions of the following hormones: (a) Parathyroid hormone (PTH) (b) Thyroid hormones (c) Thymosins (d) Androgens (e) Estrogens (f) Insulin and Glucagon 6.

The text explains that which hormonal deficiency is responsible for the following: (a) Diabetes mellitus (b) Goitre (c) Cretinism 8.

The text explains that briefly mention the mechanism of action of FSH.

The text explains that match the following: Column I Column II (a) T4 (i) Hypothalamus (b) PTH (ii) Thyroid (c) GnRH (iii) Pituitary (d) LH (iv) Parathyroid

Source: NCERT Class 11 Biology, Chapter 19: Excretory Products and Their Elimination
